title: Growing Annuity: Your friends, Lee and Kuan, are struggling to solve for...
summary: Calculate the effective annual rate or convert between APR and EAR under different compounding assumptions.
prompt:
Your friends, Lee and Kuan, are struggling to solve for the missing cash flows of
the following cash flow stream. In fact, they're convinced that "x" must be $55.87.
Note that the present value of the above cash stream with annual payments is
$6,253.75, and the discount rate is 7.2441% compounded 6 time(s) per year. This
Calculator studio solutions 1
cash flow stream pays a growing annuity starting the end of year 3 for which the
growth rate is 5.00%. Also, it pays a perpetuity starting the end of year 7.
What is the value of the missing cash flows?
solution:
Step 1 Highlight Relevant Information
Your friends, Lee and Kuan, are struggling to solve for the missing cash flows of
the following cash flow stream. In fact, they're convinced that "x" must be $55.87.
Calculator studio solutions 10
Note that the present value of the above cash stream with annual payments is
$6,253.75, and the discount rate is 7.2441% compounded 6 time(s) per year.
This cash flow stream pays a growing annuity starting the end of year 3 for
which the growth rate is 5.00%. Also, it pays a perpetuity starting the end of
year 7.
What is the value of the missing cash flows? And, are Lee and Kuan correct?
A few things to note:
The first highlighted bit (i.e., $6,253.75 represents "PVt=0"; therefore, this value
is the present value of all our future discounted cash flows
The second highlighted bit (i.e., 7.2441% represents "QR"; therefore, this figure
must be compounded 6 times per year to earn the Effective Annual Rate EAR
The third highlighted bit suggests that we're dealing with a Growing Annuity
starting the end of year 3 to the end of year 6; therefore, it'll span four 4 years
The fourth highlighted bit (i.e., 5.00% represents "G"; therefore, this figure will be
crucial to our Growing Annuity formula
The fifth highlighted bit suggests that we're dealing with a Perpetuity starting the
end of year 7; therefore, these missing cash flows will span an indefinite amount
of time (i.e., forever)
The timeline seems to be broken down into three 3 parts: two identical payments
starting at the end of year 1 until the end of year 2(i.e., Normal Annuity); a Growing
Annuity starting at the end of year 3 until the end of year 6; and a Perpetuity
starting at the end of year 7. Therefore, it may be best practice to break down this
complex timeline into its individual parts
Step 2 Building our Timelines
The timeline seems to be broken down into three 3 parts: two identical payments

year 7

Step 7 Solving for our Missing Cash Flows
So why did we do all this?
To solve for "x", our missing cash flows! Therefore, you need to do a little bit of
basic algrebra to derive "x". However, I believe it to be important to recall a few
basic TVM concepts before solving for "x"...
PV consists of the sum (Σ) of all future discounted cash flows; therefore, the PV of
our above cash flow stream will consist of the sum (Σ) of the PV our Normal
Annuity, our Growing Annuity, and our Perpetuity
